text: The surgeon carefully dissects and removes adhesions from the upper midline and epigastrium, which are remnants from a previous open cholecystectomy. The colon is visibly drawn into these adhesions. After breaking into the space above the liver edge, the surgeon reflects the omentum upwards, restoring anatomical orientation for further dissection.

The surgeon carefully dissects and removes adhesions in the upper midline and epigastrium, which are visible due to a previous right subcostal incision. The colon is drawn up into these adhesions, and the surgeon works to free it to reflect the transverse colon over the liver. The space above the liver edge is exposed, but no tattoo marking is visible. The surgeon completes the adhesion removal to restore anatomical orientation and reflects the omentum upwards.

The surgeon slows the video to normal speed to locate the tattoo and splay the omentum upwards, revealing the iliocolic pedicle. The medial to lateral dissection begins, isolating the iliocolic vessels approximately one centimeter from their takeoff. The surgeon marks the path of the superior mesenteric vessels to avoid skimping on the mesentery. The iliocolic vein is taken, followed by the artery. An anatomical variation is noted with the right colic vessel coming off the ileocolic, which is isolated above the bifurcation. The tattoo is identified in the retroperitoneum, and the surgeon prepares to address the middle colic vessels next.

The surgeon reflects the omentum upwards to visualize the iliocolic pedicle and begins a medial to lateral dissection. The iliocolic vessels are isolated approximately one centimeter from their origin, with attention to the path of the superior mesenteric vessels. The surgeon identifies an anatomic variation with a vessel branching from the iliocolic, revealing the right colic vessel.

The surgeon identifies the right colic artery branching from the ileocolic artery, creating a window above the bifurcation for safer dissection. The tattoo marking is visible in the retroperitoneal space. The surgeon performs medial to lateral dissection, splaying the transverse colon to locate the root of the middle colic vessels, and marks the mesentery to the planned distal transection margin left of the middle colics.

The surgeon performs medial to lateral dissection, splaying the transverse colon to locate the root of the middle colic vessels. The mesentery is marked to the planned distal transection margin, just left of the middle colics. The surgeon thins out the mesentery at the root of the middle colic vessels, identifying and taking the middle colic artery and vein. The dissection reveals the back of the stomach, and the surgeon takes down the mesogastrium from an inferior approach, protecting the duodenum with a sponge. The stomach is visible due to adhesions from a prior cholecystectomy, and the omentum is split at the planned transection level.

The surgeon thins out the mesentery at the root of the middle colic vessels, encountering thicker tissue. After identifying the middle colic artery and vein, the surgeon dissects a branch of the loop of Henle. The procedure continues from a medial position, taking down the mesogastrium from an inferior approach, revealing the back of the stomach.

The surgeon places a sponge to protect the duodenum and continues dissecting the distal margin mesentery. The proximity of the stomach is noted due to adhesions from a previous cholecystectomy. The surgeon splits the omentum at the planned transection level, identifying the stomach and re-entering a previously dissected plane. The dissection progresses laterally, taking down the mucogastrium and gastrohepatic omentum, including the gastroomental vessels.

The surgeon continues to dissect the mucogastrium and gastrohepatic omentum, moving laterally and encountering the gastroomental vessels. The hepatic flexure dissection is visible, having been completed earlier in the procedure. Adhesions around the appendix from a previous appendectomy are addressed, and the terminal ileum is freed to facilitate lateral mobilization, reaching the distal transverse colon for anastomosis.

The surgeon completes the lateral dissection, addressing adhesions around the appendix from a previous open appendectomy. The terminal ileum is carefully freed from retroperitoneal attachments, ensuring the ureter is not involved. The colon is fully mobilized, and the small bowel mesentery is taken. The surgeon checks perfusion and prepares to staple from the suprapubic port for specimen extraction.

The surgeon identifies sutures from a prior appendectomy and proceeds to detach the lateral attachments of the colon, ensuring careful separation of the terminal ileal attachments from the retroperitoneum to avoid involving the ureter. The ureter is visualized and preserved during this process. The colon is now fully mobilized, and the surgeon prepares to divide the small bowel mesentery, checking perfusion before stapling from the super pubic port.

The surgeon places the resected specimen above the liver and aligns the bowel for an isoparastaltic anastomosis. The anastomosis is constructed using a fully sewn technique, starting with an outer back row of sutures, avoiding the use of staplers for the anastomosis construction.

The surgeon places the resected specimen above the liver and aligns the bowel for an isoparastaltic anastomosis. The surgeon begins sewing an outer back row to construct the anastomosis manually, avoiding the use of staplers.

The surgeon performs a colotomy and enterotomy, initiating the inner row of sutures for a two-layer colorectal anastomosis. The posterior staple lines are joined to form the posterior outer row, using hand-sewn techniques for the extracorporeal anastomosis. The second inner suture is started in the opposite direction to ensure full closure of the mucosal edges.

The surgeon creates a colotomy and enterotomy, beginning the inner row of sutures for a two-layer colorectal anastomosis. The posterior staple lines are joined to form the posterior outer row, using hand-sewn techniques for the extracorporeal anastomosis. The second inner suture is started in the opposite direction to ensure complete closure of the mucosal edges.

The surgeon checks the perfusion using ICG imaging as they come around the corner of the bowel. The anterior inner row of the anastomosis is completed by canalizing over the top, avoiding knot placement at the ends. The surgeon joins two sutures to finish the common channel and uses the original suture to sew anteriorly for the back row.
The surgeon checks the perfusion using indocyanine green (ICG) imaging and begins sewing the anterior inner row of the anastomosis, completing the canalization over the top.

The surgeon adjusts the original suture to move the knot away from the corner of the anastomosis, then joins two sutures to complete the common channel. The original suture is used to sew anteriorly for the back row.
